speaker: interviewee
text: I told you about the fact that we came to Hamburg. We were taken to Hamburg. We didn't do anything but had been done to. And there were SS people on the cars at first. And then all of a sudden, they disappeared. So we couldn't understand what was happening. Some people started saying, maybe they're going to kill us now. Because there are no SS around. But instead, the Red Cross came onto the train and said that we are free. And we are going to be taken to, to Denmark. We were taken to Denmark, gotten off the trains. And we were walking down. We must have looked most terrible. Because the Danish people were lining the streets. And I could see in their faces that we looked terrible. Because they went like this. And they started throwing-- they themselves had wooden shoes on-- the good shoes, they threw to us. And they, they were just so friendly. After, after the first shock, they had smiles and tried to encourage us and so on. We were taken to some kind of a camp. I don't know if it was a school or whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or where it was. You don't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the town, I don't know. Denmark, that's the only thing I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said that we have food ready there. But everybody can only take one portion. So we thought, my gosh, what's the difference between the Germans and, and, and the Danes? They won't give us food. So people tried to go in for more than one portion. The reason was that we were so starved, and our stomach just weren't strong enough to deal with too much food. But we didn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people went for more and more portions. And many of them died. And many of them had dysentery. And you just walked in it all. It was a most horrifying sight, really terrible. But they had no control over us. We were thousands and all starved. As a matter of fact, I insisted on having more than one portion. And I did. But thank God, nothing happened. My sister usually, and my friends, took care of, of getting me things. And they did. Anyway, so we were in Denmark. And we had a cursory examination, not very carefully. Then we were put on boats. And we were taken to Sweden, to Helsingborg. And there, they put us into schools. I think it was kind of a campus type of thing. And there, they gave us, new clothes, and took all our things. And now I can never forgive myself. I have no time to tell about this. I had something from home. I had a kerchief from home, from my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I just didn't save it at all. And they gave us new clothes. And they gave us food. They told us to be very careful and examined us and then announced all the people who have to-- about all the people who have to go to hospitals. Well, I looked fairly good-- comparatively speaking, good. And my sister was very weak and thin, so the doctor came out to say Lili Fuchs has to go to sanatorium. So we both said, you mean Suri-- Sara, she said and I said. No, Lili. Said, but but no. We says, you must have made a mistake. Well, it turned out that I had TB. And she was, thank God, in good shape. So I was taken to sanatorium in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Halberstam, [? Halberstam. ?] And it was a school. It was summer, so they gave school over to make it-- they made it into a hospital. And my sister was taken to [? Brederwed ?], to a small town, where she was in-- put in quarantine. And there were many people, of course, had TB, very bad cases. And, and many of them died. I didn't have too bad a case. I was there for about eight, nine weeks. And then I was taken to [? Brederwed, ?] where my sister was not in quarantine anymore. But there was no place to be, so it was a kind of a camp. But there was food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in touch with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know where she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We knew. We knew. We corresponded. And, and we-- and she came to visit me once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day you corresponded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we corresponded, yeah, at least a few times a week. And I must say that for some reason-- I don't know. With all this that we went through, I still insisted on being religious. I ate the food, because I knew enough to know that you can't do that, being sick. But I never did anything on Shabbat. And my sister and I corresponded. I never wrote a letter on Shabbat. I just wanted to be sure that I will not succumb and not going to let Hitler triumph. And then at one point a 'rabbi'-- quote, unquote-- came to speak to us, to the hospital. And there were many Swedes who figured, here are all these young girls. We can choose one to adopt. And many of them did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But some of the girls had misgivings. Because after all, they are not Jews. So they checked with the rabbi, asked him, is it all right to be adopted by these non-Jews? So he says, of course. Go right ahead. Because you have no family, no one to take care of you. How are you going to support yourself? And these people will love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when he said that, I couldn't understand what kind of a rabbi he was. I just knew there was something wrong. Turned out, he was a reform rabbi. And as a result, many of the Jews who survived-- many of the Jewish girls who survived-- ended up in Sweden, marrying non-Jews. And that was the end of that. So Hitler did triumph there. After that, I was taken to where my sister was, [? Brederwed ?], which was this camp. And some girls were working already, including my sister. She did some things, too. And it was getting to be already close to the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the war over by now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. The War was over. And as a matter of fact, when we were in Sweden already, I still have a, a newspaper saying the war is over. When was it? Was it the May 5th or something? Or 6th, something like that. But I was in Sweden already. And then the Jewish community in Sweden started to organize themselves. And there were many people started all kinds of schools. We were all young. [INAUDIBLE] started schools, and the [? Hakshiva ?], you know, preparation to go to Israel. And then there was one school which was started-- we didn't know which school was which-- but it just turned out that the one we chose was the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is right before the holidays, you said, you chose--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We chose that school. We didn't know but one to the other, from the other. And it was the best school, which was-- they decided to teach us all to become teachers. And they taught us-- well, most of us had only a elementary school education. And the girls from, from Poland didn't even have a, an, a, an elementary school education. And they started from scratch and just taught us all the subjects.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Swedish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hebrew, by the way, [INAUDIBLE]. In Hebrew, they taught us history, geography, and, and algebra, and geometry, all the subjects of high school, and of course, Hebrew subjects, Hebrew and, and dikduk. You know, what you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Grammar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grammar. And Chumash and Rashi and Tanakh and all these things to become teachers. And we were there for three and 1/2 years. And at that point, by the way, I knew Hebrew better [LAUGHS] than I know now after having lived in, in Israel for 17 years. Anyway, I knew Hebrew, but a different kind. This was-- now is more spoken Hebrew that I know. Anyway, and we had a very, very good background. Well, not to take too much time, I just want to say that the school, as a school you know, to continue learning, went to Israel. But by that time, my sister was in America. I wanted to go to Israel all the time and my sister, never. She didn't want to go. And we didn't want to separate. And then she went to America, because it looked like the whole school will be going to America. And she had her papers ready, so she just kind of preceded us. But turned out that the rest of the school went to Israel. And my father said not to separate. And I came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when did he tell you not to separate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we found him after the war. And we corresponded with him. And correspondence, he told us not to do it. So I came to America. By that time, we were separated anyway. Because she was married by the time I got here. And she was living in Chicago while I was living in New York. So I went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well, it was [? Beit ?] [? Yaakov ?] brought students over, you know, to learn there. And I was one of the students. They brought me over as a student. And the HIAS gave me $65 per month, which included food, lodging, clothes, transportation, everything $65. Well, you can imagine, I wasn't swimming in money. But I did live all right. I didn't take [LAUGHS] buses too easily. I didn't write letters to easily. It was, it was three pennies. It was very expensive.

speaker: interviewer
text: For a stamp, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Anyway, I was studying. I continued at [? Beit ?] [? Yaakov ?]. And I, I came in November of '48, 1948, November. And I started-- I went to night school for English [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to Hebrew school, you know, to seminary, at [? Beit ?] [? Yaakov. ?] Beginning January, I went to the [? Beit ?] [? Yaakov ?] high school and continued the seminary. Then in one year-- they wouldn't graduate me because they wanted me to have residence, you know, for one year. After a year, I graduated both seminary and secular high school with all the Regents Exams--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: In English, which I didn't know. [LAUGHS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of those good things that started to happen to you? What are some of the good things that happened after the war to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well, the fact that I had a chance to get an education, which I always wanted. I was able to actually finish high school and get a degree as a teacher in Hebrew. Well, that was a big accomplishment for me. I signed up for college immediately. And of course, I did not finish immediately, I can assure you. Because I got married a year and a half after I arrived. And then I just went to-- wherever I happened to be, I went to college. And it took me till 1964, from 1950-- I graduated high school in 1950. In 1964, I got a BA. Finally. [LAUGHS] It was a long BA. Then again, years later, I went back for my masters. But all this with a family and married, so it wasn't, wasn't easy. But this is what I-- this was very important to me. It was very important. So I, I did get it, but I can tell you one thing, I would not go for a PhD, [LAUGHS] absolutely not. I won't go near a university. [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what about your children? You had family and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about them was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank God I had, I have three really wonderful children. And I have how many grandchildren? I have a new one now. So it's-- four-- seven grandchildren. Really, one nicer than the other, one better than the other, one nicer than the other, good looking, and of course, the most brilliant in the whole world, wouldn't you know it? [LAUGHS] No, they're really good students and good children. And this is, this is quite an accomplishment, as far as I'm concerned. That was very important, very important that Hitler did not triumph. He did triumph because just think, if each of my sisters and brothers would have had seven grandchildren, that's quite a few. The only-- my only-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my biggest regret is that my children are not religious, [CRYING] two, two out of the three. And that to me is very, very important, very important. And I really feel like [HEBREW SPEECH], God forbid, God forbid, Hitler might triumph after all. [CRYING] I have such wonderful children, such wonderful grandchildren, such wonderful, wonderful ones. [CRYING] But it's just impossible to remain a Jew not being religious, really impossible. And being a Jew is the most important thing to me. In my whole life, in my whole life, I don't have any, any desire to be a good American, a good anything, just a good Jew. I was a loyal American. I worked here. I contributed here. But the only loyalty I have is the Jewish-- to the Jewish people and to Israel. And if this is not continued, it would-- it's the biggest tragedy in my life-- the second biggest, [CRYING] having lost my family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [HEBREW SPEECH] God forbid, God forbid, I-- that would be the biggest, biggest tragedy that could happen to me. And I don't think much about this, because they're such wonderful children. And I appreciate them so much. And you can see how beautiful they are. You can see. And [SIGHS] OK, I just hope they'll remain Jews. That's all. That's my biggest, biggest hope for them. Because I see already they are intelligent and ambitious. And they can accomplish a lot. But to remain Jews, that's very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Lili.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK, well this is my family here in the States, except for my son-in-law. I'm very sorry he's not here. This is my daughter, Zahava, my oldest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these are the-- my three grandchildren. And I've told Jonathan many times nobody can replace his-- him being the oldest grandchild. And nobody can replace Sara being the oldest granddaughter. And this is Ari.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rest of the family is in Israel. I have two sons in Israel-- Eliot. And he has two children. His wife is Hadas, and Shlomi and Pnina. And David is married to Amira. And he has Tamir, Tamiri, and, [LAUGHTER] and Noam. And I'll show you pictures afterwards. But at this point, maybe the children want to say something. OK, well, I told you before that they are good looking. I wasn't lying, right? So here they are. You certainly weren't. You certainly weren't. Well, perhaps you'd like to tell us what it was like growing up in the home of a survivor of the Shoah, if it had an impact on you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in 1953. And it really wasn't so very long-- I realize now-- after my mother was liberated from the camps. And what really, [SIGHS] what's really striking about the way I grew up is the fact that, when I was little, my mother must have talked to me a lot about, about everything that happened. Because I grew up, I grew up knowing it and feeling as though I had been there. I knew many, many, many details of the things that happened to her. My mother stopped talking about these things when I was five or six years old. I must have been such a tiny child when she, when she talked about them. And yet, I remember everything perfectly. I even, I even know what it looked like. I, I grew up knowing what it looked like, what it was like. [SIGHS] And she stopped. And then when I was much older, just a couple of years ago, she began talking about it. It was as though I, I knew. I had known it all along.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know that I insist there is ESP? Because I never talked.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I knew about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. And she knew about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was-- I was determined not to talk. Because I heard some of the other people who did it. And I didn't want to impose on my children. But she knew about it anyway. This is-- there's osmosis. There's ESP. There are so many things. Because by the time she became conscious, which was about five or six, she thinks I stopped. Fact is, I never started.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's so interesting. Oh, it would be--

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew all the incidents you told us about. Everything, I knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: This would be-- this would warrant--

speaker: interviewer
text: Further investigation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Investigation. This is the most bizarre thing. OK, now the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, what's it like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonathan?

speaker: interviewer
text: Jonathan, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Um--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sara, I know that you also interviewed your grandmother. And what was it like for you, honey?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was interesting. I learned a lot. And then it influenced me to learn even more. I started reading books. I just finished reading Anne Frank's diary. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This must be the second time you read it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the first. But I've read lots of other books.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's read lots of work about the diary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? she. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's becoming an expert on World War II.

speaker: interviewee
text: Really.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And Jonathan, have you read anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I, I read a few of those Holocaust books.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonathan does a great deal of reading. So does Sara. They have their mother to take after. She reads vast amounts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Arie?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Ari.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ari.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ari. Excuse me. Ari, it's nice that you're here. You all came after school. Do you have anything to say about what, what it was like and having a grandmother in Israel who was in concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sort of feel proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I feel proud of Ari, Jonathan, and Sara-- no end. Of course, Zahava was always wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ari was named the after my mom's uncle, [? Leibzisa ?], her favorite uncle, who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who died in the war. Sara was named--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was killed in the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was killed in the war. Sara was named for Aunt Suri--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who survived the war, who I'm sure you've heard a lot about in this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Jonathan was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was named--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Yosef.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was named-- he's Yosef.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Yosef?

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he was his father's mother's father, so his maternal grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks all of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [SIGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Kiss. Kiss, kiss. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I won't give you a kiss, no, no, no. It's hard to kiss--

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I won't even touch Ari-- I mean, Jonathan. I won't even touch him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I love him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I love both of them and all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lili, tell us about these pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my great-grandfather. To the left is my great-grandfather, who came to Israel in 1900. And this is one of his two sons next to him. He came with two sons. And this is his daughter-in-law. And I grew up with this picture because he must have sent it to all his grandchildren because we had it in my house. And I thought I will never, never see it again. But I found it in Israel, because that's where he lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Chaim Steiner. And his son's name is Dovid Leib Steiner. And her name, I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my little brother, Haimi. His name was Alter Chaim. It was taken as a school picture, very, very tiny. And it was enlarged. That's why it looks so grainy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- somebody who went back found it. He must have been about 10 here. But he was about 14 when he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was-- that's it. This is Gitu, my younger sister. And this is also an enlargement from a school picture, from class picture. And I got it in 1991 when I went back to my hometown. And I found a classmate of hers who had this picture. And I had it enlarged. And she was about 12 when, when she was killed. Maybe-- This is my sister, Piri. She was the youngest of the four girls. And this is in Vyskovo, where we were all born. And she also must have been nine, 10 years old. And it was taken as a school picture, the same way as my other sister's picture was. And I found it in, in town at some school, schoolmates' of hers. And they gave it to me to enlarge. This is a picture of me, to the right. I was 14. And these are two cousins in the next town, [INAUDIBLE], where I spent some time. And my two cousins were-- one of them must have been about 11, the older one to the left. And the younger one was about seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name and her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Chaim. Also we have Chaims because of this great-grandfather of mine. And Ruchi-- I don't know after who she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Farkas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is a picture of my father, who survived the war. His name is Shmuel Aaron Fuchs. And it was taken in Israel, because he moved to Israel in 1955. This is my sister to the left, Suri, after whom my granddaughter was named, Sara. And that's her husband. And she died in 1978 of cancer. This is my brother, Berel. It was probably in 1945. He died in 1946 after the war, near Munich. He survived, but was too sick. He had TB and something with his stomach. [SIGHS] These are my three children, Eliot to the left. He's-- he lives in Israel. He's the head of the fingerprinting lab of the Central Police State, whatever. And the second one is David, who also lives in Israel. And he works in ELOP, if you know about that. It's a government company, and Zahava, whom you saw, who lives right here, and [? Sarale, ?] the little one who you also saw. These are my two daughters-in-law, Amira to the left, and Hadas to the right. It was on Amira's wedding. And Amira has two children now, and Hadas also. This is Steve to the right and Jonathan and Eliot to the left. They were in Israel, Steve and Jonathan, at that time. These are my six out of seven beautiful grandchildren, and I love them all. They are--
